author: @Guardian_Arias
text: You don't need a pillar drill if you have a 3d printer. You can just print a cuboid with a hole in the middle and then press a bushing into it. Very gently sand the top to be used as reference and it should be well enough accurate with a hand drill. The top and not the bottom because the first layer references off your bed level but the hole will be parallel to the print head and the top layer will be perpendicular to the print head also. Unless you have bed level compensation on without knowing and your cuboid is taller than where the bed level compensation fades away.

author: @Guardian_Arias
text:
Regarding 3D printers, my advice is once you have determined an ender 3 or a clone is extremely useful and something you can actually use then look into a Voron or CoreXY equivalent and or a resin printer especially since resins and FDMs are as similar to each other as a router CNC machine and a Laser CNC machine.

But I 100% agree the top tier tool you need is a dedicated work area and that includes either a bench or a desk. But a pillar drill might be overkill for someone who is asking what tools do I need to get tinkering. 

A bench vise is extremely useful but thanks to my career in aviation tech/engineering I've been forced to live without and therefore I simply just haven't bought one for my shop even though I'm sure I could pick up an old rusted one for free that's more robust and functional than a new one.

In the woodworking side of YouTube, the site is littered with what tools does a beginner need and they start naming all kinds of things and quickly rack up a bill that's several thousand USD. When the answer should be get a handheld circular saw, a hand drill, a basic drill bit kit, a basic driver kit, Titebond III 16oz bottle, some deck screws, either a basic square or angle finder a level, food grade mineral oil, make-up safe beeswax and that's it. This grocery list should come under 500 USD even if you splurge a bit on all of it. This actually leaves you a budget to then make your way out of your favorite big box hardware store to a local dedicated lumber distributor where you can buy good quality hardwoods for almost the same as the cheap crappy pine you probably would have bought or overpriced pretty wood. Most of them will plane and joint the edges for you for still less than a board of barely usable pretty wood from a big box store. You can now make tables, beds, shelves, picture frames, jewelry boxes, children toys, entertainment centers, swing sets (rope not included), and so much more. If you can't or don't like it then it's not for you.

welp....this is why you don't do comment in little bits at a time over a few hours.

Do not buy Hammer Drills or Impact Drivers combo kits just get a single  runner up top tier compact hand drill in your favorite color. The second tier compact drills often come bundle with a runner up circular saw almost like they know whats what.

author: @brawndo8726
text: I like to think of 3D printed parts as a type of glue or binder for off the shelf parts.  There's quite a bit more to designing for FDM due to printing challenges and the strength orientation of the layer lines, but in general, I approach the design process as creating brackets for holding other materials relative to one another.  This philosophy also helps save time and money printing shapes that could be replaced with a common material.  For example, I was designing a windmill blade using acetate sheets where the printed part was simply a means of shaping the blade and pinning it to its neighboring blades and shaft (I really should dig that project out again).

replies.text: good ay of looking at it mate

Thank you. I have many interests, and have spent the last few years gathering a literal shedload of equipment and tools.. aside from a viable workspace (and now a bl**dy 3D printer 😅) ..I have everything I “need” to get started.

I made a small start on my workspace today, it wasn’t easy, and it left me feeling there’s an insurmountable mountain to climb.

This video has given me the ‘gee up’ I needed. 

Much appreciated *tips hat* 

My only criticism of your video’s is the sound quality.. you often sound like you’re shouting across the room.. I struggle to hear you as clearly as other content makers, I just wish I could provide suggestions for mic/ settings to help with this.

Did you ever have this experience when driving, the need to turn the radio down to see better?

When I listen to you, I need to squint.. I struggle with focus, let alone many of the concepts you present ..but I do appreciate the entirety of your channel. 

Your content is like an idiots guide to x,y, and z.. I’m struggling with the ABC’s tbh 😬 

Thank you for the inspiration 
*doffs cap*

author: @DKFX1
text:
Would be interesting to hear what about the 3D printer you find so attractive or effective. 
Does it do jobs that you couldn't do without it, can it do it faster and prettier? These are the questions that are really at the core of the utility of tools.

replies.author: @skampp
replies.text:
From my usage, there are quite a few things that I made that otherwise I couldn't due to the shape of them.  Also I have extended the functionality of basic tools with it.  I have a drill press rig for my dremel.  I have made a threaded locking base for my DeWalt DW-660 so that it now can operate as a router.  I've printed the base parts for a 1m x 1m CNC / 3D Printer / Laser Cutter.  I've replaced highly specialized parts of all sorts.  I make concrete molds for flower pots which is fun.  Heck, I'm even printing the thrust bearings and using BB's for the bearings because I can't find the exact one in the States that Robert uses for his latest things.  I already print my own skater bearings for fairly low to medium speed things.  But often if the shapes are basic enough or if you are more skilled than I am (which isn't hard), you can just as easily use builder's board or lumber.  I was designing another gearbox and thought, this would be so much quicker with wood due to the simplicity of the design.  3D printing is, in general, pretty slow.  All that being said, I wholeheartedly agree with Robert's assessment that it is an essential tool when you can get smaller ones for < $100.  But I would recommend getting one with the largest bed you can afford.

So your questions.. Does it do jobs that you couldn't do without it?  Yes on occasion but not daily.  Can it do it faster?  Probably not for most things, but if it's the only way you can make an item that really doesn't matter much.  Can it do it prettier?  Oh for sure, yes.

Hope this helps.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i have said it quite often mate in many videos - you might want to review them as the answer is long and involved and multifaceted - but  basically comes down to they are super useful for a wide number of people with a variety of skill levels and budgets both in time cost and space

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: @skampp Sounds like a lot of fun. I own a 3D printer myself so I'm well aware of the many possibilities, but as always it would be great to hear Roberts thoughts on those questions as well. However your input is appreciated, seems you've been putting yours to good use! ;)

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Absolutely, I heard a few short comments about it in a couple of videos. The content is top notch so don't take my comments as criticism ;)

author: @leslieapplegate65
text: I'm in the process of cleaning out the big barn and reorganizing it. My dumpster is full...and it's full of trash - all of the useful stuff and recyclable things are in other places. My goal is to have the workshop on one side and the livestock stuff on the other side. Getting there slowly....

replies.text: nice - i would love a big barn!!

author: @industrialmonk
text:
Hi the first metal tools were made with stone tools & the first lathe/machine tools were made by a blacksmith using the tools he made & the second lathe was made with the first lathe always looking for better quality so you can start with nothing/ old free tools at no cost other than a little time.
Also I still use a 1950s avo multimeter as it's easier & quicker than a digital one & was free ( I do have fluke/lab stuff as well) but simple old is still valid.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate

replies.text: I’ve been looking for at least 8 months just to FIND a 3D printer I can use in my area.  Here on the Big Island of Hawaii theres no maker spaces or anything like that yet that I’ve found.  Things can be a little slower to take hold here I suppose…

replies.author: @Nick_Tag
replies.text: @maukaman use a service like shipito to get one sent over . . i got a second hand ender 3 for 90 quid and it's better than a new one as all the parts were upgraded and bootloader / firmware already installed for me.

author: @Rei_Nori
text:
You are right, a bench is a tool. I do not know how it is in the UK, but here in the US, you can pick up used solid-core doors for the price of a latte. They make brilliant bench tops at ~38 mm thick with a nice finish ply on the outside. Dead flat and stiff enough to get very level. I also lucked into a pool (billiard?) table that had water damage on the legs.... That is a bench top.... 

Love your channel. One of the reasons I watch is that my shop is >150' from the electrical service. So getting it off-grid would be wonderful.

replies.author: @Struthio_Camelus
replies.text: You can find solid-core doors slabs? Where?

replies.author: @In.Darkness
replies.text: Very cool!

replies.author: @Rei_Nori
replies.text:
Salvage/recycle places often have them. Blows and seconds are often used as dunnage to ship cabinets without some ham-fisted forklift driver from messing them up.

For used doors, do look at the nameplate on the hinge side of the door. If a wood-faced door is rated over one hour, it is likely cored with asbestos insulation. Back out a screw and if it is white on the threads, walk away.

replies.author: @campbellpaul
replies.text: If you don't have much space for a full size bench, a Workmate can do wonders..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @funkysod
text: So you ended the lease on the workshop? The one thing you need more than space is time. Time, for me,  is bought by cutting expenses. So in my view this is a good move. These days there's a lot of talk, everything is expensive etc.. But one good thing is that we are forced to think about what we consume and what we really need. Good for the soul, creativity and ultimately the planet. Thanks Rob!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to paraphrase Shakespeare - you can buy anything with time - and i think you are right mate - we need to drop instant gratification as an idea

replies.text: @chrishutton1458 One man's clutter might be an other's organization system. His quote didn't mean live in clutter, it meant find your inner peace. For example find a video or an image of Adam Savage's workshop. At a glance it might look cluttered but the layout has reason with utility and ease of access as a priority.
